html:
Calibration axis
Demo steps
⚠ Figure failed to load

elements: 3D world origin O with labeled coordinate axes: X_W (blue), Y_W (red), Z_W (green), arrows, and axis labels
Image plane: gray transparent rectangle, slanted and offset from world origin, labeled 'Image plane'
Camera center (projection center) O, shown as a sphere or point at the origin
Three distinct vanishing points: vX (blue dot), vY (red dot), vZ (green dot), located on the image plane
World tick marks along each axis: spheres/cylinders at integer multiples of alpha_X, alpha_Y, alpha_Z, labeled (e.g. '2α_X')
Projected tick marks: circles/dots on image plane corresponding to the perspective image of each world tick
Projection (dashed) lines from each world tick mark to its projected image plane location
Reference object segments: vertical segment (bookshelf, for Y, length 1.97m, red), circular segment (table base, for X or Z, radius 0.5m, blue/green), labeled with real-world length
Horizon line on the image plane: gray dashed line spanning vX and vZ
Ground plane: faint grid/rectangle in 3D intersecting the axes and image plane for context
Axis-angle contextual reference: faint arrows/arcs showing the NON-orthogonal projection of the axes onto the image plane
Annotation overlays: dimension labels for tick distances, projected tick positions, and vanishing points

interactions.label: Axis to Calibrate
interactions.teaches: Switches the axis being calibrated; lets you see how perspective shapes each axis and projects its ticks differently according to vanishing points and reference measurement.

interactions.label: World Ticks
interactions.teaches: Shows more or fewer world tick marks (and their projections), emphasizing how image tick spacing compresses with distance and diverges with proximity due to perspective distortion.

interactions.label: Reference Size (meters)
interactions.teaches: Sets the real-world distance spanned by one tick step (alpha_X, alpha_Y, or alpha_Z) based on a chosen reference; demonstrates how known scene measurements calibrate metric reconstruction.

interactions.label: Axis Angle (degrees)
interactions.teaches: Rotates the chosen axis away from its canonical 3D orientation, to match the original figure's perspective and demonstrate how axis direction affects vanishing points and projected tick spacing.

interactions.label: Image Plane Tilt (degrees)
interactions.teaches: Tilts the image plane around the world Y axis; lets you explore the effect of non-orthogonal imaging geometry on the position and scale of axis projections and vanishing points.

demo_steps.title: World and Image: Y Axis Calibration
demo_steps.narration: Let's start by calibrating the Y axis, using the bookshelf as our reference (1.97 meters tall). Five world ticks are placed in 1-meter steps along Y. Observe how the projected tick marks on the image plane become visually closer together as they move farther from the camera, illustrating strong perspective compression.

demo_steps.title: Perspective: X Axis and Vanishing Points
demo_steps.narration: Switch to the X axis and use the table base radius (0.5 meters) as the reference. Notice how the projection lines for X angle dramatically off to their blue vanishing point, and that the tick marks project far less evenly than for Y. Each axis's perspective foreshortening depends on its direction and vanishing point.

demo_steps.title: Increasing Tick Density and Reference Size
demo_steps.narration: Now increase to 9 ticks on the Z axis with a reference of 2 meters. Distant ticks seem to pile up at the green vanishing point. This shows how large physical intervals crowd together on the image — direct evidence that tick placements must use the cross-ratio, not even image spacing.

demo_steps.title: Adjusting Axis Orientation: Perspective Effects
demo_steps.narration: Rotate the Y axis away from vertical and tilt the image plane. Watch the vanishing points, axis angles, and projected ticks shift accordingly. This matching of perspective to the real camera/view is crucial for aligning calibration to real images as in the original textbook figure.

demo_steps.title: Key Insight: Why Tick Marks Are Not Even
demo_steps.narration: No matter which axis or measurement, projected tick marks are never evenly spaced in the image. Their locations depend on world geometry, axis angle, image plane tilt, and vanishing points. Metric axis calibration needs correct cross-ratio reasoning — not intuitive spacing.

plan.camera_suggestion: Oblique isometric view showing all three axes, vanishing points, the image plane at a visible angle to the axes, and ground plane grid for context. Zoom so full extent of world ticks, projected image ticks, reference objects, and vanishing points are in view. Optionally, a 2D inset view of just the image plane as seen from the camera for projected tick spacing clarity.
plan.notes: Axis orientation (axisAngle) and image plane tilt (imagePlaneTilt) must directly modify both the axis directions and the image plane placement to match the geometric relationships in the reference figure. When the axis or image plane is rotated, recalculate and redraw vanishing points, tick projections, and all connecting rays. Color code axes and projected ticks consistently. Use depth cues (shadows, faint ground) to visually separate 3D and projected points. Reference object segments should highlight which measurement governs the active axis. Make transitions between steps smoothly animate all affected elements.

plan.contextChunk:
Simply placing them at evenly spaced intervals along the axis in the
image won't be correct. Due to geometric distortion introduced by
perspective projection, the tick marks are not evenly spaced within the
image plane. This distortion affects each axis in a distinct manner.

Let's start assuming we know the location of some arbitrary 3D distance
to the origin on each axis that we will denote by $\alpha_X$, $\alpha_Y$
and $\alpha_Z$. The question is, where do we place the ticks for
$k\alpha_X$, $k\alpha_Y$ and $k\alpha_Z$ for all $k$? We will use the
cross-ratio to calibrate the projection of the world coordinate system
in the image plane. For doing this we will need the location of the
horizon line of a reference plane (i.e. the ground plane), the position
of the orthogonal vanishing point and the real measure of one object in
the scene.

As shown in @fig-axis_calibration_procedure (a) we can use the cross-ratio
to estimate the location of the points $Y= k \alpha_X$ where $\alpha_X$
is an arbitrary constant as shown in @fig-axis_calibration_procedure (b),
and the corresponding image location $r$, for $k=1$, is chosen
arbitrarily as shown in @fig-axis_calibration_procedure (a). Due to
perspective projection, the image projection of the point
$Y= k \alpha_X$ will not be evenly spaced in the image. In this
particular example we chose the orientation of the world axis in image
coordinates to be aligned with the location of the vanishing points.

![Using the cross-ratio invariant to project the world coordinates into the image using only the vanishing points.](figures/single_view_3d/axis_calibration_procedure.png){width="100%" #fig-axis_calibration_procedure}

In order to calibrate the axis we need to know the dimensions of an
object in the scene. We need measurements along the three axis. In this
example, we used the bookshelf height (197 cm) and the radius of the
table base (50 cm). Using these reference measurements we can solve for
the image locations of the ticks $\alpha_X=\alpha_Y=\alpha_Z=1$ m.

@fig-office_all_calibrated_box shows the final calibrated axes with
ticks placed each 50 cm along each of the three axes.

![Calibrated world axes. Each axis is calibrated using the measurement of a scene element parallel to the world-coordinate axis. In this example, we used the bookshelf height (197 cm) and the radius of the table base (50 cm). From the calibrated axes, you can see that the office is around 250 cm wide.](figures/single_view_3d/office_all_calibrated_box.png){width="100%" #fig-office_all_calibrated_box}

From this calibrated system we can directly read the height of the
camera. The location of the camera coincides with the location of the
horizon line because, in this example, the ground plane is horizontal.

elements: 3D world coordinate axes: X_W (blue), Y_W (red), Z_W (green) with arrows and axis labels
Image plane rectangle at a slanted angle in space
Projection center (camera center) at the origin O
Dotted projection lines from each 3D point to the image plane
3D world points: P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4 as black spheres
Image of axes/points projected onto the image plane
Vanishing points v1 (blue), v2 (green), v3 (red) shown as colored dots in both 3D and image
Axis tick marks in world: integer multiples of alpha_x, alpha_y, alpha_z, labeled
Projected tick mark locations in image plane and as 2D dots on the right diagram
World reference measurements: bookshelf height segment (Y), table base radius segment (X or Z)
Horizon line of the ground plane (dashed or highlighted line on the image plane)
Image plane annotation ('Image plane')
Ground plane for context (can be a faint square)

interactions.label: Select Axis
interactions.teaches: Switches which world axis is calibrated and whose ticks are visualized, demonstrating that each axis is affected differently by perspective.

interactions.label: Number of Ticks
interactions.teaches: Increases the number of ticks drawn along the chosen axis in world and image, showing how spacing in the image compresses with distance due to perspective.

interactions.label: Reference Size (m)
interactions.teaches: Changes the world distance between ticks by setting the reference object size used for calibration; this gives intuition for metric reconstruction from known real measurements.

demo_steps.title: Projecting Y axis with perspective
demo_steps.narration: Let's start by calibrating the Y axis using the bookshelf as a reference object. We set the reference size to 1 meter and show 5 ticks, each corresponding to 1 meter step along the Y axis in the world. Notice how the tick spacing on the image plane becomes more compressed farther from the origin due to perspective.

demo_steps.title: Switch to X axis calibration
demo_steps.narration: Now, switch calibration to the X axis, using the table’s radius as the 1 meter reference unit. The positions of vanishing points and the image tick marks shift. As before, ticks are not evenly spaced on the image plane because perspective distorts metric distances differently along each axis.

demo_steps.title: Increase number of ticks
demo_steps.narration: Increase the number of ticks to 8 along the currently selected axis. In the image plane, the additional tick marks appear even more crowded at greater distances from the camera, highlighting how perspective projection compresses the apparent spacing of distant objects.

demo_steps.title: Adjusting reference measurement
demo_steps.narration: Change the reference size to 2 meters for the Z axis. Now, tick marks are spaced farther apart in world space, but their projections on the image still crowd together. This shows why accurate metric calibration requires both a known measurement and careful use of the cross-ratio with vanishing points.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Oblique 3D view showing both the world coordinate axes extending from the origin and the image plane at an angle; zoomed so all vanishing points, the full image plane, and at least five ticks per axis are visible. Secondary top-down or front view for the 2D image plane inset.
interactionPlan.notes: When axisChoice changes, update tick marks, projection lines, and highlight the current axis in both world and image plane views. Show projection from each tick to the image plane as dashed lines. (For the 2D image plane inset, overlay projected axes and image-plane tick marks including vanishing points.) Use color-coding consistent with the axes for clarity. Animate tick appearance and axis highlight smoothly during step transitions.

attempts.html:
Select Axis
Demo steps
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The 3D axes are not aligned correctly with the image plane as in the source.
The vanishing points are incorrectly positioned.
Tick mark placements and labels are inconsistent with the source.
Rotation of the image plane differs.
Colors of the axes are slightly different.
evaluation.notes: The 3D geometric arrangement and axes orientations have visible discrepancies.
action_items: Align the 3D axes more accurately with the original image plane orientation.
Correct the placement and visibility of vanishing points and ticks.
Adjust scale and representation of objects to better match the source.

feedback.rationale: The discrepancies and action items suggest that the issues are execution-based, such as alignment, positioning, and scaling, which can be resolved by refining the generation rather than changing the plan.
action_items: Align the 3D axes more accurately with the original image plane orientation.
Correct the placement and visibility of vanishing points and ticks.
Adjust scale and representation of objects to better match the source.
actionItems: Align the 3D axes more accurately with the original image plane orientation.
Correct the placement and visibility of vanishing points and ticks.
Adjust scale and representation of objects to better match the source.

discrepancies: Images are incorrectly positioned between world coordinates
Vanishing points are not correctly aligned with axes
Tick labels positions do not match original
evaluation.notes: Configuration of figure has wrong angles and perspectives, but interactions are well-implemented.
action_items: Adjust the angles of the axes so they match the original perspective.
Reposition the image planes to align correctly with world coordinates.
Correct position and scales of vanishing points and tick marks.

feedback.rationale: The failure modes indicate fundamental issues with depth, wrong primitives, and scaling — suggesting the conceptual understanding in the plan needs correction to achieve proper layout and structure before generation refinements will be effective.

Correct position and scales of vanishing points and tick marks.
